planting.note: Propagate by air-layering or semi-hardwood cuttings (not from seed — this sterile hybrid sets no viable seed) or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; Hong Kong orchid tree is frost-sensitive and should be protected from temperatures below 28°F — established specimens in zone 9 may recover from the roots if frozen back.

directions: Propagate by air-layering or cuttings (this sterile hybrid sets no seed) or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Hong Kong orchid tree is a sterile hybrid flowering tree and the floral emblem of Hong Kong — one of the most spectacular flowering trees for zone 9–12, producing enormous, deep-rose-purple to magenta-crimson, orchid-like flowers up to 6 inches across that appear in great abundance from autumn through late spring on the arching branches, creating a brilliant and prolonged display of large, showy, fragrant tropical flowers at the cooler end of the year. The large, uniquely bilobed, rounded, butterfly-wing leaves (characteristic of all Bauhinia species) are attractive year-round. Because it is a sterile triploid hybrid, it does not set seed and has no invasive potential — unlike many other Bauhinia species that self-seed aggressively — making it the preferred choice for ecological responsible planting. It flowers for the longest season of any Bauhinia and is widely considered the most ornamental. Tolerates partial shade acceptably. Excellent as a specimen or small shade tree in the tropical or subtropical garden.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds (nick seed coat with a file or soak 24 hours in warm water before sowing to improve germination) or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun in spring; purple orchid tree germinates readily from seed and self-seeds moderately in warm climates — remove unwanted seedlings before they establish.

directions: Sow scarified seed or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in full sun; purple orchid tree is a South Asian deciduous to semi-evergreen flowering tree widely planted throughout zone 9–12 landscapes for its large, showy, fragrant, five-petaled, orchid-like flowers in shades of pink, lavender, purple, and white with crimson veining that appear in autumn through early winter — uniquely, the tree flowers most abundantly after dropping its large, rounded, bilobed leaves in late fall, so the spectacular flower display occurs on nearly bare branches for maximum visual impact. The flowers are followed by flat, woody seed pods that persist through winter. Bauhinia purpurea flowers in fall-winter (August–December in the Gulf South), while the related B. variegata flowers in late winter-spring — together they extend the Bauhinia flowering season. The young leaves and flower buds are edible and eaten in Indian and Southeast Asian cooking. It is one of the most reliable and easily grown flowering trees for zone 9–12 and germinates readily from seed, though seed-grown trees vary in flower color and intensity.

planting.note: Direct-sow scarified seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, poor to average soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; Mexican bird of paradise thrives in hot, dry conditions and poor, rocky, calcareous soil — rich, moist, heavily fertilized soil produces rank foliage growth at the expense of flower production.

directions: Sow scarified seed or plant container specimens in well-drained, poor to average soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican bird of paradise is a Mexican native evergreen shrub or small tree in the pea family (Fabaceae) that produces upright racemes of bright, clear-yellow, five-petaled, ruffled flowers with showy long, red stamens on a nearly continuous basis from spring through frost — a bloom season that, combined with its tolerance of zone 8 cold and adaptability to dry, alkaline, calcareous soil, makes it one of the most reliably free-flowering large shrubs for Gulf Coast and southern Texas landscapes. It differs from the related red bird of paradise (Caesalpinia pulcherrima) in producing all-yellow flowers (vs. orange-red), being significantly more cold-hardy (to approximately 15°F in zone 8), and having a tidier, less aggressive growth habit. The fine-textured, bipinnate, ferny, dark-green, evergreen leaves remain attractive year-round. It tolerates partial shade significantly better than most drought-tolerant flowering shrubs. Excellent as a specimen, informal hedge, and xeriscape flowering tree in zone 8–11.

planting.note: Direct-sow scarified seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to rocky, poor soil in full sun in spring; fairy duster is intolerant of clay, wet, or rich soil — it thrives in the harshest, driest, most alkaline desert and rocky foothill conditions and declines quickly in moist, fertile, or amended garden soil.

directions: Sow scarified seed or plant container specimens in well-drained, sandy to rocky, poor soil in full sun; fairy duster is a low-growing native Sonoran and Chihuahuan Desert shrub in the pea family (Fabaceae) — one of the most charming and ecologically valuable small flowering shrubs of the desert Southwest — producing masses of powder-puff-like flower heads composed entirely of long, filamentous, pink-white to deep-pink stamens that give the plant its evocative common name. The silky, feathery flower clusters appear in late winter through spring, just as desert wildflowers peak, and provide an exceptionally early nectar source for emerging queen hummingbirds, sphinx moths, and native bees. The delicate, twice-pinnate, tiny-leafleted, gray-green foliage is drought-deciduous (dropped in extreme drought) and creates a fine, airy texture. Fairy duster grows naturally on dry, rocky, desert foothill slopes and bajadas and demands perfectly drained, lean, rocky soil — it fails in amended or moist garden soil. Its cold-hardiness to approximately 10°F makes it the most cold-adapted of the calliandras, suitable through zone 7. An excellent xeriscape shrub for dry Southwest gardens.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade after frost; dipladenia performs best in consistently moist but well-drained soil — waterlogged roots cause yellowing and dieback, while drought stress causes bud drop and leaf loss.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, fertile, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; dipladenia (mandevilla, rocktrumpet) is a Brazilian tropical twining vine widely sold throughout the garden industry as a patio and container flowering plant for its large, trumpet-shaped, glossy-petaled flowers in vivid shades of red, deep-pink, rose-pink, and white, produced continuously throughout the warm season on new growth. It is one of the most extensively-sold container flowering vines in the American nursery trade, marketed under the names dipladenia (for the more compact, less-twining forms) and mandevilla (for the larger, more vigorous climbing forms). The glossy, deep-green, oval leaves and shining, varnished-looking flower petals give the plant a distinctive, tropical-polished appearance. As an outdoor perennial in zone 10–12 it grows vigorously on trellises and arbors; in cooler climates it is used as a summer annual or overwintered as a container plant indoors. The compact cultivars (sold as 'Dipladenia') are bushy and non-vining; the larger forms (sold as 'Mandevilla') climb to 10 feet or more. The plant exudes milky sap; wash hands after pruning.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
